macro_segments.title: Mixed Sacrifices: When a Male Asham Gets Mixed With a Shelamim—Rabbi Shimon's Lenient Approach Versus the Rabbis' Stringent Position

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah's Case: Asham Mixed With Shelamim and the Two Competing Solutions
Rabbi Shimon's Solution: Slaughter Both in the North and Have Male Kohanim Eat Them
The Rabbis' Objection: The Problem of 'Bringing Sacrifices to the House of Invalidity'
The Rabbis' Alternative: Wait for a Blemish, Then Redeem and Repurchase

macro_segments.title: The Concept of 'Ain Mevi'im Kodshim L'Beis Hapesul' (Not Bringing Sacrifices Into a Context of Invalidity): The Shvi'is Money and Terumah Precedent

micro_segments.title: The Baraita's Teaching: You Cannot Buy Terumah With Shvi'is Money Because It Limits the Time of Eating
Rava's Distinction: Rabbi Shimon Only Allows This When There's Already a Problem (Bedi'eved), Not Lechatichilah

macro_segments.title: Abaye's First Challenge to Rava: The Mishna of Tavlin (Spices) Shows Rabbi Shimon Allows It Lechatichilah

micro_segments.title: The Mishna: Kohanim Can Eat Sacrifices Any Way, Including With Terumah Spices
The Proof: Throwing Terumah Onion Into a Chatas Stew Limits Its Eating Time, Yet Rabbi Shimon Permits It

macro_segments.title: Abaye's First Challenge to Rava: The Mishna of Tavlin (Spices) Shows Rabbi Shimon Allows It Lechatichilah (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rava's Dismissal: That's Only Rabbinic Terumah, Not Biblical

macro_segments.title: The Concept of 'Ain Mevi'im Kodshim L'Beis Hapesul' (Not Bringing Sacrifices Into a Context of Invalidity): The Shvi'is Money and Terumah Precedent (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rava's Qualification: Only When the Restriction Is Rabbinic, Not Biblical

macro_segments.title: Abaye's Second Challenge: The Mishna of Shvi'is Vegetable Cooked in Terumah Oil Shows Rabbi Shimon Allows Limiting Biblical Sanctity

micro_segments.title: Rav Yosef's Question: Why Didn't You Challenge Rabbah From This Shvi'is Vegetable and Terumah Oil Case?
Abaye's Answer: I Already Asked From the Tavlin Case and Rabbah Dismissed It as Rabbinic
Rav Yosef's Rebuttal: The Oil Is Biblical Terumah—This Should Have Been a Stronger Proof That Rabbi Shimon Allows It Lechatichilah
The Back-and-Forth on Whether the Vegetable Case Can Be Dismissed as Bedi'eved: Abaye's Explanation and the Counterargument

macro_segments.title: The Case of the Metzora With Doubt: The Asham That Might Be a Shelamim, Brought With Stipulations and Nesachim

micro_segments.title: The Scenario: A Man Who Might Be a Metzora Brings an Asham With an Oil (Log) and Makes Stipulations
The Stipulation Process: If He's a Metzora, It's an Asham; If Not, It Becomes a Shelamim Nedavah
The Slaughter and Ritual Procedure: Shechita in the North, Blood Application, Semicha, and Nesachim
Abaye's Challenge: This Mishna Shows Rabbi Shimon Allows Limiting a Shelamim's Eating Time Lechatichilah

macro_segments.title: The Halachic Problem of the Oil: If the Man Is Not a Metzora, How Can the Oil Be Used as a Korban?

micro_segments.title: The Question: What Sanctification Status Does the Log of Oil Have If It's Not Needed for a Metzora Ritual?
The Stipulation Solution: Sanctify the Oil With a Condition—If Metzora, It's Metzora Oil; If Not, It's a Voluntary Mincha-Like Offering
The Procedure: Take a Handful (Kmitzah) for the Altar Fire, Then Refill and Perform the Metzora Ritual With the Remainder

macro_segments.title: The Halachic Prohibition Against Using Remnant Oil for Ritual: The Problem of 'Kol Shememenu L'Ishim Harei Hu B'Val Taktiru'

micro_segments.title: The Rule: Once You Burn a Portion of a Korban-Designated Item on the Altar, You Cannot Use the Remainder for Another Ritual
The Dilemma: If You Take the Kmitzah First and Burn It, Then the Remaining Oil Is 'Remnant' and Cannot Be Used for Sprinkling
Rashi's Interpretation: The Sprinkling on the Metzora Is Treated Like a Korban Offering, So the Same Prohibition Applies

macro_segments.title: The Solution: Treating Non-Designated Oil as 'Fuel' or 'Water'—Rabbi Yehuda Bar Rabbi Shimon's Loophole

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Eliezer's Principle: You Can Burn Something on the Altar as Fuel (Not as a Korban Offering) Even if It Normally Would Be Prohibited
The Application to Our Case: If the Man Is Not a Metzora, We Treat the Oil Like Fuel (or Water) So It Doesn't Violate the Prohibition

macro_segments.title: The Solution: Treating Non-Designated Oil as 'Fuel' or 'Water'—Rabbi Yehuda Bar Rabbi Shimon's Loophole (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rashi vs. Tosafot on What the Loophole Covers: Sprinkling (Rashi) vs. Burning on the Altar (Tosafot)

macro_segments.title: The Halachic Prohibition Against Using Remnant Oil for Ritual: The Problem of 'Kol Shememenu L'Ishim Harei Hu B'Val Taktiru' (continued)

micro_segments.title: Tosafot's Alternative: The Prohibition Is About Burning Remnant on the Altar Fire, Not About Sprinkling

Log (Measure of Oil for Metzora Ritual)
Kol Shememenu L'Ishim Harei Hu B'Val Taktiru (Remnant Prohibition)
